childhood pastor always quoted Luke's gospel in asking,<|12.80|> <|13.40|> did not our hearts burn within?<|15.76|> <|17.02|> And then he'd follow up with a short sermon himself,<|20.72|> <|21.20|> ongoing song, a music, a poem sung, sustained.<|17.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When he sinks into a vision of the ancient temples sanctum sanctorum or holy of holies, the prophet Isaiah overhears music being sung betwixt the seraphim, six winged angelic creatures.<|18.70|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And in the King James Version, it says,<|2.54|> <|2.54|> "With twain they covered their faces, and with twain they covered their feet, and with twain they did fly.<|11.44|> <|11.44|> And one cried unto another and said, 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts.<|19.76|> <|19.76|> The whole earth is full of His glory.'"<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It has long been the sense of Christians, East and West, Catholic, Orthodox, Protestant, that this is the song constantly sung in the presence of God.<|16.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The lyrics are familiar to us because they are sung at every Mass, every day and week,<|9.24|> <|9.24|> here at St. John's Cathedral and across the world.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> When we sing, "Holy, holy, holy Lord, God of power and might,"<|20.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we are in a mystic union with what one hymn writer calls the music of the spheres.<|10.00|> <|12.00|> The lion was pacing to and fro about that empty land and singing his new song.<|20.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was softer and more lilting than the song by which he had called up the stars and the sun, a gentle rippling music.<|9.50|> <|9.50|> And as he walked and sang, the valley grew green with grass.<|14.50|> <|14.50|> It spread out from the lion like a pool. It ran up the sides of the little hills like a wave.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In a few minutes, it was creeping up the lower slopes of the distant mountains, making that<|6.50|> <|6.50|> young world every moment softer.<|10.00|> <|11.46|> Thus opens chapter nine of The Magician's Nephew, prequel to The Lion, the Witch, and<|18.62|> <|18.62|> the Wardrobe, my favorite childhood novel in C.S. Lewis's Chronicles of Narnia series.<|26.36|> <|26.36|> It's<|27.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Here, Aslan births Narnia through music and song.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> Vigangoroyan, the Arminian Orthodox theologian that I seemingly cannot not quote in my sermons,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> connects Narnia's origin story to the creation stories in Genesis.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There he says, "I think of the first chapter of Genesis as a divine litany."<|7.70|> <|7.70|> And litany is just a call and response that we do in prayer, usually set to song in church.<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Something is happening and then God says as a respondent, he takes both the role of liturgist and respondent.<|7.38|> <|8.40|> This is good after every day of creation.<|13.72|> <|14.66|> This is good after every day of creation.<|17.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Now, notably, a friend pointed this out last week.<|3.00|> <|3.00|> The only day, and this is not a joke, the only day that God does not say, "This is good" at the end of that day is Monday.<|14.00|> <|18.00|> Read your Bible when you get home. You'll see that it's not there.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that friend's recommendation, as is mine, is that you really need to love Mondays because if you don't, you're hating one-seventh of your life.<|12.02|> <|13.86|> So God says at the end of every day of creation, in sort of a litany, this music, This Is Good, that,<|23.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And in the Greek translation of the Old Testament, that word "good" is translated "beautiful."<|8.68|> <|8.68|> Beautiful. This is beautiful.<|12.26|> <|12.26|> Aslan sings Narnia into being and God sings our universe into being.<|19.94|> <|19.94|> This music, this song is unceasing, sustained.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Perhaps liturgies like this one and exercises like prayer are ways for us to tune our hearts to the key of God.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> But what key does God sing in, Dr. Boney?<|15.00|> <|15.00|> How can I bring myself to identify the eternal song when I hear it?<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It might sound something like the well of enslaved Israelites in Exodus and more recently the well of enslaved Africans on this continent crying out for deliverance from the grip of commodification.<|16.40|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It might sound something like the blues of the Mississippi Delta, the bluegrass of Appalachia, or New Orleans bounce.<|9.34|> <|9.34|> It might sound like the rest of pleas of children and youth demanding safe schools or laborers calling for equitable wages and equitable working conditions.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or it might sound something like Al Green playing on Saturday while you're cleaning your house,<|6.00|> <|6.00|> or a soulful Roberta Flack and Donny Hathaway duet.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> I don't know much, but the music of the spheres,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> the song by which God stirred newborn stars and all things came to be,<|23.00|> <|23.00|> the full-toned resonant note at the navel of existence,<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This music has an endless combination of key through every genre, on every corner,<|8.64|> <|8.64|> in every home, by every race, in every socioeconomic class, in every career path,<|15.50|> <|15.50|> on every job, beneath, above, and inside of every suffering, blessing, triumph, mistake.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But again, the ears of our hearts must be attuned to hear it.<|8.04|> <|9.40|> The music can be heard in the crackling wood of an old rugged cross,<|16.54|> <|17.50|> weighed low by our fleshy incarnate God in birdsong and urban traffic<|24.14|> <|24.14|> near a tomb emptied of desperation but full of hope.<|29.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And the song of a young single pregnant mother on occupied land in her moan too deep for<|6.96|> <|6.96|> words at her young, at her son's dying feet.<|12.64|> <|12.64|> And the space between her scarce final breaths as his familiar voice welcomes her home.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> It can be heard in the moment after the bread is placed in your palm, a fleeting cradle of the infinite.<|9.00|> <|10.72|> This bread, grace upon grace, is irresistible.<|16.70|> <|17.92|> And how do I know it's irresistible?<|19.74|> <|19.96|> Because y'all won't stop coming to church.<|22.48|> <|22.48|> Amen.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "Oh no," I tell my colleagues, a little embarrassed.<|5.66|> <|5.66|> St. John's Cathedral doesn't take a summer vacation from Jesus.<|10.72|> <|10.72|> I said something like this to a friend who was here last Sunday, and he texted back,<|16.84|> <|16.84|> and I have his permission to share this, "Well, home is home.<|23.26|> <|23.26|> You have renovations, but you still got to eat."<|27.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So feast, feast, and in your feasting, you'll hear the music. Amen.<|10.38|><|endoftext|>
